name: Dumbbell bench press
howTo: Lie flat on the bench, a dumbbell in each hand above your chest, palms forward.
Lower under control until your elbows sit at ~45° to your torso.
Press back up; stop just short of lockout. Don't clang the dumbbells at the top.
what: The foundational dumbbell chest builder. Longer range of motion than a barbell and forces each side to stabilise independently.
tips: Feet planted, slight lower-back arch. 2s down, 1s up.

name: Incline dumbbell press
howTo: Set the bench to ~30°. Sit back with a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height.
Press the dumbbells up and slightly in, palms forward.
Lower under control until your upper arms match the bench angle.
Press back up without flaring the elbows out.
what: Shifts emphasis onto the upper chest and front delts. The 30° angle is the sweet spot — any steeper and it becomes a shoulder press.
tips: Keep the dumbbells on slightly angled tracks, not a straight vertical line.

name: Dumbbell fly
howTo: Lie flat on the bench, dumbbells pressed above your chest, palms facing each other.
With a soft bend in the elbows, open your arms out to the sides in a wide arc.
Stop when your upper arms are level with the bench — you should feel a stretch across the chest.
Squeeze the chest to bring the dumbbells back together over your chest.
what: An isolation move for the chest that hits it through a long stretch. Use lighter weights than you think.
tips: Hug-a-tree motion. If your elbows straighten, the weight is too heavy.

name: Dumbbell pullover
howTo: Lie across or along the bench, holding one dumbbell with both hands above your chest.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, lower the dumbbell back over your head in an arc.
Stop when you feel a deep stretch through the chest and lats.
Pull back to the start by driving the elbows down toward the hips.
what: An old-school move that works the chest and lats at the same time through a huge stretch.
tips: Ribs down, core braced. Don't let the lower back arch off the bench.

name: Dumbbell floor press
howTo: Lie on your back on the floor, knees bent, dumbbells at your shoulders.
Press the dumbbells up until arms are nearly straight.
Lower until your upper arms touch the floor, pause for a beat.
Press back up.
what: The floor stops the elbows early, which makes it easier on the shoulders and shifts more work onto the triceps.
tips: Good substitute when no bench is available.

name: Bent-over row
howTo: Stand with feet hip-width, a dumbbell in each hand.
Hinge at the hips until your torso is ~45° above parallel, back flat.
Row the dumbbells toward your hips, elbows driving back, not out.
Lower under control, keeping the torso still.
what: The bread-and-butter back builder. Hits the mid-back, lats and rear delts all in one.
tips: Don't jerk the torso up to help — if you have to, lighten the load.

name: Single-arm row
howTo: Place one knee and hand on the bench, the opposite foot on the floor, back flat.
Hold a dumbbell in the free hand, arm hanging straight down.
Row the dumbbell up to the side of your ribs, elbow close to the body.
Lower all the way down for a stretch. Repeat, then switch sides.
what: Braced-on-the-bench version lets you focus on one side at a time and move more weight safely.
tips: Don't rotate the torso to lift heavier — keep the shoulders square.

name: Chest-supported row
howTo: Set the bench to ~30–45°. Lie face-down, chest on the pad, a dumbbell in each hand.
Let the arms hang straight down.
Row the dumbbells up, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Lower under control back to the stretch.
what: The bench takes the lower back out of it, so you can hammer the upper back without cheating.
tips: Think of the hands as hooks — pull with the elbows, not the biceps.

name: Renegade row
howTo: Get into a push-up position with hands gripping two dumbbells, feet wide for balance.
Brace the core and row one dumbbell up to your hip, keeping the hips square.
Lower it back down with control.
Alternate sides, one row per side.
what: A row and an anti-rotation plank in one. Brutal on the core if you do it strictly.
tips: Hexagonal dumbbells are safer — round ones roll. Feet wider = easier.

name: Standing shoulder press
howTo: Stand tall, dumbbells at shoulder height, palms forward, elbows under the wrists.
Brace the core and press the dumbbells straight up until arms are nearly locked.
Lower under control back to the shoulders.
what: The pure, standing overhead press. Core braces hard to stop you leaning back.
tips: Glutes squeezed, ribs down. If the lower back arches, the weight is too heavy.

name: Seated shoulder press
howTo: Set the bench upright. Sit with dumbbells at shoulder height, back against the pad.
Press the dumbbells up overhead, stopping just short of lockout.
Lower back to shoulder height under control.
what: The seated version takes the core out of it so you can focus purely on the shoulders and push more weight.
tips: Back flat against the pad — don't arch off it at the top.

name: Arnold press
howTo: Sit upright with dumbbells at shoulder height, palms facing you (like the top of a curl).
As you press up, rotate the palms to face forward.
Finish with arms nearly locked, palms out.
Reverse the rotation on the way down.
what: Presses through a longer range of motion and hits the front and side delts more evenly than a straight press.
tips: Smooth rotation, don't snap the wrists. Lighter than a standard press.

name: Lateral raise
howTo: Stand tall, dumbbells at your sides, a slight bend in the elbows.
Raise the arms out to the sides in a wide arc, leading with the elbows.
Stop when the upper arms are at shoulder height.
Lower under control — no dropping.
what: The side-delt isolator. Gives the shoulders their width. Go lighter than your ego suggests.
tips: Pinky slightly higher than thumb at the top — like pouring from a jug.

name: Front raise
howTo: Stand tall, dumbbells in front of the thighs, palms facing down.
Raise one or both arms straight out in front of you to shoulder height.
Lower under control back to the thighs.
what: Isolates the front delts. Often skipped because pressing already hits them — use sparingly.
tips: No swinging. If you can't control the descent, it's too heavy.

name: Rear-delt fly
howTo: Hinge at the hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor, back flat.
Let the dumbbells hang below you, palms facing in, slight elbow bend.
Raise the arms out to the sides in a wide arc, squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Lower under control.
what: The rear delts get ignored and it shows — this one hits them directly and improves posture.
tips: Think wide, not high. Lead with the pinkies, not the thumbs.

name: Dumbbell shrug
howTo: Stand tall, a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Shrug the shoulders straight up toward the ears.
Hold for a beat at the top.
Lower slowly back down.
what: Direct trap work. Boring but effective for building that yoke above the shoulders.
tips: No rolling the shoulders — straight up and down.

name: Bicep curl
howTo: Stand tall, dumbbells at your sides, palms facing forward.
Curl the dumbbells up toward the shoulders, elbows pinned to the ribs.
Squeeze at the top.
Lower all the way back down — full stretch.
what: The classic. Nothing fancy, but does the job if you keep strict form.
tips: No swinging the torso. Elbows don't drift forward.

name: Hammer curl
howTo: Stand tall, dumbbells at your sides, palms facing in (neutral grip, like holding a hammer).
Curl the dumbbells up keeping the palms facing each other.
Squeeze and lower under control.
what: Targets the brachialis (the muscle under the biceps) and the forearms. Builds thicker-looking arms.
tips: No wrist rotation. Strict and slow on the lower.

name: Incline curl
howTo: Set the bench to ~45°. Sit back, dumbbells hanging straight down at your sides, palms forward.
Curl the dumbbells up without letting the elbows drift forward.
Squeeze at the top, lower to a full stretch below.
what: The incline stretches the biceps at the bottom — brutal for the long head.
tips: Don't swing the elbows forward to help. Lighter than you'd think.

name: Concentration curl
howTo: Sit on the bench, legs wide. Hold a dumbbell in one hand, elbow braced against the inner thigh.
Curl the dumbbell up toward the shoulder, squeezing hard at the top.
Lower under control to a full stretch. Switch sides.
what: Arnold's favourite. The braced elbow removes all the cheating and gives a deep bicep squeeze.
tips: Slow, strict, one side at a time. Quality over weight.

name: Overhead tricep extension
howTo: Stand or sit tall. Hold one dumbbell with both hands above your head, arms straight.
Keeping the elbows close to the head, lower the dumbbell behind you.
Stop when your forearms are parallel to the floor.
Extend back to the top by squeezing the triceps.
what: The overhead position stretches the long head of the triceps — the bit that gives the arm its size from behind.
tips: Elbows point up, not out. Ribs down — don't arch the back.

name: Skullcrusher
howTo: Lie flat on the bench, dumbbells held above the chest with straight arms, palms facing each other.
Keeping the upper arms still, bend the elbows to lower the dumbbells toward the sides of the head.
Extend back to straight arms by squeezing the triceps.
what: Direct tricep work that also hits the long head through a solid stretch. Named for obvious reasons.
tips: Elbows don't flare out. Light weight until the groove feels right.

name: Tricep kickback
howTo: Hinge at the hips, a dumbbell in one hand, back flat.
Tuck the upper arm against your ribs, forearm hanging down.
Extend the elbow back until the arm is straight, squeezing the tricep.
Return under control. Switch sides after the set.
what: Finishing move for the triceps. Works them in a fully shortened position where most presses don't.
tips: Upper arm stays still — only the forearm moves.

name: Goblet squat
howTo: Hold one dumbbell vertically at your chest, cupped under the top plate.
Feet shoulder-width, toes slightly out.
Squat down with the chest up until the elbows touch the knees.
Drive through the mid-foot to stand back up.
what: The easiest way into heavy squatting. The front-loaded weight forces an upright torso and a deep, clean squat.
tips: Elbows inside the knees at the bottom. Knees track over the toes.

name: Bulgarian split squat
howTo: Stand a couple of feet in front of the bench, facing away. Place the top of one foot on the bench behind you.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Lower the back knee toward the floor, keeping the front shin near vertical.
Drive through the front heel to stand back up. Switch legs after the set.
what: One of the best single-leg builders. Punishes the quads and glutes and exposes any weakness between the two sides.
tips: Front foot far enough forward that the knee doesn't crash over the toes.

name: Romanian deadlift
howTo: Stand tall, dumbbells in front of the thighs, palms facing you.
With a soft bend in the knees, hinge at the hips and slide the dumbbells down the fronts of the legs.
Stop when you feel a deep stretch in the hamstrings — torso roughly parallel, back flat.
Drive the hips forward to stand back up, squeezing the glutes at the top.
what: The best dumbbell hamstring builder. Teaches the hip hinge, which underpins everything else.
tips: Hips back, not down. Back flat, not rounded. Dumbbells stay close to the legs.

name: Walking lunge
howTo: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides. Stand tall with feet together.
Step forward with one leg and lower until the back knee nearly touches the floor.
Drive through the front heel to come up and bring the back leg through into the next step.
Alternate legs as you walk.
what: Builds the legs and gets the heart going. Also ruthless for balance and coordination.
tips: Big steps — short ones turn it into a quad-only move and stress the knee.

name: Step-up
howTo: Stand in front of the bench, a dumbbell in each hand.
Place one foot flat on the bench. Drive through that heel to step up until both feet are on the bench.
Step back down with the same leg you started with, under control.
Finish all reps on one side, then switch.
what: Single-leg quad and glute builder. The step-down is where the real work happens.
tips: Don't push off the floor with the trailing foot — the lead leg does all the work.

name: Calf raise
howTo: Stand tall, a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Rise up onto the balls of the feet as high as you can.
Pause for a beat at the top.
Lower slowly to a full stretch at the bottom.
what: Direct calf work. Needs a lot of reps and a full stretch at the bottom to actually do anything.
tips: Stand on the edge of a step for more range if you can. Slow eccentric.

name: Russian twist
howTo: Sit on the floor, knees bent, heels down or lifted for more challenge.
Hold one dumbbell at your chest. Lean back until you feel the core switch on.
Rotate the torso to tap the dumbbell on the floor to one side.
Come back through centre and tap the other side. That's one rep.
what: Hits the obliques with rotation — movement most core work skips.
tips: Turn from the ribs, not just the arms. Keep the chest up.

name: Weighted sit-up
howTo: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat on the floor.
Hold one dumbbell against your chest with both hands.
Sit all the way up by curling through the spine.
Lower under control back to the floor.
what: Adds load to the basic sit-up so the abs actually get a stimulus to grow.
tips: Don't yank the head. Slow on the way down — that's where the work is.
